content: Functions are the primary unit of code reuse and abstraction in JavaScript. Everything in Angular — components, services, guards, interceptors — is ultimately built from functions or classes whose methods are functions. Understanding the different function syntaxes, parameter patterns, and how
binding works is essential before working with Angular's TypeScript codebase.

attrs.alt: JavaScript functions: anatomy, three syntaxes, parameters, this binding, higher-order and pure functions

content: A function declaration defines a named function. Declarations are
— the entire function (name and body) is moved to the top of its scope before execution, so you can call it before the line it is written on.
A function expression assigns a function to a variable. The variable is hoisted (as
with
or into the TDZ with
) but the function itself is not. You cannot call it before the assignment.
Arrow functions are concise function expressions with a key difference: they do
have their own
— they inherit it from the surrounding lexical scope. They also lack their own
object.

content.text: When to Use Arrow Functions vs Regular Functions
Callback passed to map, filter, forEach, etc.
Defining object methods that need their own this
Inline handler in Angular template context
Constructors (arrow functions can't be used with new)
Class method that references this in async callbacks
Recursive function needing a stable name

content.text: A rest parameter collects all remaining arguments into an array. It must be the last parameter.

In Angular components and services,
always refers to the class instance when using arrow functions for callbacks. Regular method definitions in a class body also bind correctly when called as
A higher-order function either
a function as an argument or
a function. Higher-order functions are everywhere in Angular: RxJS operators, array methods, and Angular's DI factory providers are all higher-order functions.

content.text: A pure function satisfies two rules: given the same inputs, it always returns the same output (determinism), and it produces no observable side effects (no mutation, no I/O, no randomness).

content: Angular pipes are pure by default — they only re-run when the input changes by reference. If a pipe reads external state (current user, current time), mark it
. An impure pipe re-runs on every change detection cycle, which is expensive.

content.text: An IIFE runs immediately after definition. Before ES modules, IIFEs were used to create private scope. Today they appear mainly in build output and for once-only async execution.

content: Generator functions (
) produce an iterator that yields values one at a time. They are used in Redux-Saga and occasionally in custom Angular resolvers or infinite scroll patterns.

Passing a class method as a callback (
Because functions capture their surrounding scope, you can use them to create specialised versions of a more general function — a pattern called currying or partial application:
TypeScript supports function overload signatures — multiple type signatures for a single implementation. This is purely a compile-time concept; only one function runs at runtime.

content: An
function always returns a Promise. Inside it,
pauses execution until the awaited Promise resolves, without blocking the main thread. Async functions combine well with
for error handling.
Angular's HTTP layer is built on RxJS Observables, not Promises. For most Angular code, use Observables. Convert to Promise with
or
only when you need to bridge to code that expects a Promise (tests, external libraries, Angular Signals).

content.text: Pure functions are the easiest to test — no setup, no mocking, no cleanup:

content: JavaScript provides three function syntaxes: declarations (hoisted, named), expressions (not hoisted), and arrow functions (concise, no own
). Arrow functions are ideal for callbacks in Angular because they capture
from the class instance. Parameters can have defaults, use rest syntax (
), and be destructured directly.
binding depends on how a function is called — use arrow functions or

to fix it in callbacks. Higher-order functions (functions that take or return functions) power Angular's RxJS operators, guards, and interceptors. Pure functions — same input, same output, no side effects — are preferred for Angular pipes, reducers, and transform logic.
